did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (DenseNet201, InceptionResNetV2, ResNet50, VGG16, and Vit-b32) trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset to perform multi-class classification of melanoma and other skin lesions. It also utilized an open-source fuzzy algorithm (dupeGuru) for image similarity detection to preprocess the dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results investigating the impact of image similarity on melanoma classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It details a specific methodology involving the dupeGuru algorithm for image deduplication, describes the training of five deep learning models (DenseNet201, InceptionResNetV2, ResNet50, VGG16, and Vit-b32), and reports new performance metrics (accuracy improvements) in the Results section. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCBDC 2023), it contains original experimental findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: The Effect of Image Similarity on Melanoma Classification
journal: 2023 7th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC 2023)
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of image similarity on the performance of deep learning models for multi-class melanoma classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The researchers employed the open-source dupeGuru algorithm to identify and remove visually similar images from the training set at various similarity thresholds ranging from 60% to 100%. Five deep learning architectures, including DenseNet201, InceptionResNetV2, ResNet50, VGG16, and Vit-b32, were trained and evaluated on the processed datasets. The results demonstrated that removing highly similar images reduced performance-degrading bias and improved model accuracy. Specifically, DenseNet201 achieved the highest validation accuracy of 72.45% when trained on data with binary identical images removed, while InceptionResNetV2 performed best at the 95% similarity threshold. These findings indicate that preprocessing datasets to eliminate image similarity boosts the performance of melanoma classification models.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous cell carcinoma, None of the others)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images using the ISIC 2019 dataset. Five deep learning models (DenseNet201, InceptionResNetV2, ResNet50, VGG16, and Vit-b32) were trained to predict discrete lesion classes. Performance was evaluated using validation accuracy, with DenseNet201 achieving 72.45% accuracy at the 100% similarity threshold.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 dataset, a public repository of dermoscopic images used for skin cancer detection and malignancy assessment. It contains tens of thousands of images with gold-standard lesion diagnosis.

use.training: Used as the primary source for training deep learning models (DenseNet201, InceptionResNetV2, ResNet50, VGG16, Vit-b32). The training set was preprocessed using the dupeGuru algorithm to remove visually similar images at thresholds from 60% to 100%.
use.validation: A validation set of 5,066 labeled images was created by splitting 20% of the original dataset (after ensuring no overlap with training via similarity checks) to evaluate model performance.
use.testing: The test set of 8,238 unlabeled images was used for final evaluation via an automatic online evaluation system, though specific metrics for the test set were not reported in the paper.
use.other: Used to identify and analyze image similarity groups using the dupeGuru fuzzy algorithm.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The authors stated they 'hypothesised' that model performance would degrade due to bias from similar images, but they evaluated this through comparative accuracy metrics (validation accuracy) across different similarity thresholds rather than statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The paper reports that DenseNet201 achieved the highest validation accuracy of 72.45% when trained on data with binary identical images removed (100% threshold). InceptionResNetV2 performed best at the 95% similarity threshold with 71.52% accuracy. For non-identical images, the 85% threshold yielded the best validation accuracy of 71.64% (for DenseNet201). The paper did not report test set metrics as the ground truth for the ISIC 2019 test set was not publicly available.

DenseNet201_detailed_thresholds.notes: Table 4 provides detailed training and validation results for the DenseNet201 model across all similarity thresholds from 60% to 100%, as well as the baseline. The baseline validation accuracy is listed as 70.64% in Table 4, whereas Table 3 lists it as 70.61%; both values are reported here.

architecture_used.main_model: Five models were used: DenseNet201, InceptionResNetV2, ResNet50, VGG16, and Vit-b32 (Vision Transformer with 32x32 patches). DenseNet201 is noted as the best performing model.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the specific models in the context, though ImageNet is mentioned in related work as a common source for pretrained weights.

architecture_details.DenseNet201: Utilises an Inception module with residual connections which allows for the detection of multiple large scale features enabling the network to counter the vanishing gradient problem.

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7-6700K CPU @ 4.00GHz, NVIDIA TITAN X (Pascal) 12GB GPU, 32GB RAM

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: Multi-class classification head for 9 classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous cell carcinoma, None of the others).

preprocessing_techniques: Image similarity detection using the open-source dupeGuru fuzzy algorithm to identify visually similar images in the ISIC 2019 dataset.
Removal of similar images from the training set at similarity thresholds ranging from 60% to 100% to mitigate performance-degrading bias.
Cross-image similarity and duplicated file checks between training and validation sets to ensure no overlapping or duplication.
Splitting 20% of the dataset to create a validation set, ensuring distinctness from the training set via similarity checks.
Merging dupeGuru output CSV files (containing group IDs and filenames) with ground truth label files to filter training data.
Retention of only the first image from each similarity group ID in the new training datasets to ensure distinct examples.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used the open-source dupeGuru fuzzy algorithm to identify and remove visually similar images from the ISIC 2019 training set. Similarity thresholds ranging from 60% to 100% were applied. For each threshold, groups of similar images were identified, and all images except the first one in each group were removed to ensure distinct examples. This technique was used to mitigate performance-degrading bias caused by image duplication/similarity rather than traditional geometric or color augmentation.

conclusions: Highly similar images present in the ISIC 2019 training set result in performance-degrading bias for deep learning models.
Removing highly similar images from the training set boosts model performance.
DenseNet201 achieved the highest validation accuracy (72.45%) when trained on data with binary identical images removed (100% similarity threshold).
InceptionResNetV2 performed best at the 95% similarity threshold with a validation accuracy of 71.52%.
Different model architectures respond differently to visually similar images; some benefit from eliminating redundant images, while others may experience performance decline if too many are removed.
It is necessary to gauge the impact of similarity and establish an optimum threshold for each model and dataset to achieve successful outcomes.
The study highlights the importance of identifying and mitigating bias within datasets through the removal of similar images.
The analysis was limited by the lack of available ground truth labels for the ISIC 2019 test set, preventing the reporting of test set metrics.
important_remarks: The study is limited by the lack of publicly available ground truth labels for the ISIC 2019 test set, preventing the reporting of test set metrics and external validation.
The paper is characterized as preliminary research based solely on the ISIC skin cancer dataset, with no cross-domain validation reported.

Different model architectures responded differently to image similarity; while DenseNet201 improved, lower-performing models showed reduced performance from the 95% threshold onwards, indicating that removing too many similar images can be detrimental for some architectures.

The validation set was created by splitting 20% of the dataset, but the paper notes that cross-image similarity checks were performed to ensure no overlap between training and validation sets, addressing potential data leakage risks.
The study relies on the dupeGuru fuzzy algorithm for image similarity detection, which operates on average color blocks, rather than deep feature-based similarity metrics.
The dataset contains significant class imbalance (e.g., 12,875 Melanocytic nevus vs. 239 Dermatofibroma), which may influence the generalizability of the similarity findings across rare classes.
